1: "This is the commandment — the statutes and the rules — that the LORD your God commanded me to teach you." The entire legal corpus is framed as teaching for life in the land. Jesus positions his teaching similarly: "I am the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). What Moses delivers as curriculum, Christ delivers as embodied reality — he is not a teacher of the way but the way itself.
2: "So that you may fear the LORD your God... all the days of your life." The fear of YHWH as comprehensive life-orientation, generational and pervasive. Christ brings those who fear God into the family of the Son: "God sent forth his Son... so that we might receive adoption as sons" (Galatians 4:4–5). The reverence due to YHWH is now the reverence of children who know the Father through the Son — intimacy within appropriate awe.
3: "A land flowing with milk and honey" — the covenantal shorthand for the abundance YHWH has prepared. This inheritance is the type of "an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven for you" (1 Peter 1:4). Christ secures the eternal inheritance for those who are in him (Hebrews 9:15): what the land of Canaan represented materially, the new creation provides permanently.
4: The Shema — "Hear, O Israel: the LORD our God, the LORD is one" — is the theological center of Israelite faith. Jesus cites it as the first commandment (Mark 12:29). Paul expands it with a daring Christological insertion in 1 Corinthians 8:6: "for us there is one God, the Father... and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things." What Deuteronomy 6:4 attributes to YHWH, Paul distributes to Father and Son without abandoning the oneness — placing Jesus within the divine identity itself.
5: "You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart, soul, and might." This is the first and greatest commandment (Matthew 22:37–38). No human being has kept it fully — except Christ, who loved the Father with perfect wholeness (John 14:31: "I love the Father and I do exactly what the Father has commanded me"). Those in Christ participate in his love: "as the Father has loved me, so I have loved you" (John 15:9) — divine love moves from Father to Son to those who abide in him.
6: "These words that I command you today shall be on your heart" — the command requiring inwardness that Torah-obedience alone could not achieve. Jeremiah 31:33 makes this the content of the new covenant promise: "I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts." Christ's gift of the Spirit (John 14:26) fulfills the Torah's demand for heart-inscription by producing the inner alignment with God's will that law-keeping alone cannot generate.
7: Teaching children as they sit, walk, lie down, rise — the permeation of covenant instruction through all moments of daily life. The NT equivalent is Paul's vision of all of life oriented by the gospel: "whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do it all for the glory of God" (1 Corinthians 10:31). Christ's kingdom is not confined to formal religious settings; the parables consistently locate it in ordinary life (farming, fishing, baking, finding — Matt 13).
8: The command to bind words on hand and forehead — later interpreted as tefillin — represents the embodied, whole-person nature of covenant commitment. Paul contrasts this external binding with the new covenant's internal equivalent: "you show that you are a letter from Christ, written not with ink but with the Spirit... not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts" (2 Corinthians 3:3). What tefillin symbolized externally, the Spirit writes internally.
9: Writing the words on doorposts and gates — the mezuzah tradition — marks the covenant people's dwelling as consecrated to YHWH. Christ's prayer in John 17:15–17 consecrates his disciples in the world: "sanctify them in the truth." The community marked by YHWH's word on their doorposts is the type of the community marked by Christ's truth throughout their whole life and household.
10: "Great and good cities that you did not build" — the inheritance contains gifts already prepared. Ephesians 2:8–10 applies this grace-structure to salvation: "by grace you have been saved through faith... it is the gift of God." The inheritance of good things not personally constructed is the shape of all covenant grace. Paul adds: "we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand" (v10) — even the future fruitfulness is pre-prepared.
11: "Houses filled with all good things that you did not fill" — grace as reception rather than achievement. This is the kingdom posture: the wedding banquet is already prepared (Matthew 22:1–14); the nets are filled by the word of Christ (Luke 5:4–7). Christ fills his people with what they could not provide: "from his fullness we have all received, grace upon grace" (John 1:16). The land's pre-filled houses are the type of the pre-prepared grace the gospel announces.
12: "Take care that you do not forget the LORD who brought you out of the land of Egypt." Prosperity creates the danger of amnesia — when all needs are met, the Giver becomes invisible. The Lord's Supper is Christ's institution of structural remembrance against the same danger: "do this in remembrance of me" (Luke 22:19; 1 Corinthians 11:24–25). The Passover memorial against forgetting the Exodus is fulfilled and surpassed by the Eucharist as memorial against forgetting the new exodus in Christ.
13: "You shall fear the LORD your God; you shall serve him and swear by his name." Jesus quotes this directly in Matthew 4:10 / Luke 4:8 when refusing Satan's temptation to worship him: "it is written, 'You shall worship the Lord your God and serve him only.'" This is one of three Deuteronomy texts Jesus deploys in his wilderness trial. His use reveals that he understood his vocation as the true Israel who would succeed where the nation failed — passing the test by embodying Torah-loyalty.
14: "You shall not go after other gods, the gods of the peoples who are around you." The exclusivity of covenant allegiance. Christ claims the same exclusive devotion: "whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me" (Matthew 10:37); "no one can serve two masters" (Matthew 6:24). The command against idolatry is not softened in the new covenant but sharpened — now the one whom absolute loyalty is owed has a name and a face: Jesus Christ.
15: "For the LORD your God in your midst is a jealous God." YHWH's jealousy is covenantal possessiveness — his refusal to be one option among others. Paul applies the jealousy-of-God language to the church: "I am jealous for you with a godly jealousy; I promised you to one husband, to Christ" (2 Corinthians 11:2). The jealous God of Sinai is the jealous bridegroom of the new covenant, protecting the covenant bond against spiritual adultery.
16: "You shall not put the LORD your God to the test, as you tested him at Massah." Jesus quotes this verbatim (Deuteronomy 6:16) in Matthew 4:7 / Luke 4:12 when refusing the temptation to jump from the temple pinnacle. This is the second of his three Deuteronomic responses in the wilderness trial. The original Massah incident (Exodus 17:1–7) was Israel doubting YHWH's presence; Jesus refuses to manufacture a crisis to force a display of divine protection — he trusts without demanding proof.
17: "You shall diligently keep the commandments of the LORD your God." The Deuteronomic emphasis on diligent obedience sets a standard the nation consistently failed to meet. Christ kept the commandments diligently and completely (Hebrews 4:15: "without sin"; 2 Corinthians 5:21: "he who knew no sin"). His perfect diligent obedience is the ground of justification for those in him: "by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous" (Romans 5:19).
18: "You shall do what is right and good in the sight of the LORD." The summary ethical command — righteousness and goodness as YHWH defines them. Christ fulfills this by being himself the righteousness of God: "he is the source of your life in Christ Jesus, whom God made our wisdom and righteousness and sanctification and redemption" (1 Corinthians 1:30). What Israel was called to be by commandment, Christ is by nature and act.
19: "By thrusting out all your enemies from before you, as the LORD has promised." The promise of victory over enemies is the type of Christ's final conquest (Revelation 19:11–21; 1 Corinthians 15:25–26: "he must reign until he has put all enemies under his feet; the last enemy to be destroyed is death"). The Canaanite enemies thrust out before Israel anticipate the powers — sin, death, the devil — thrust out by Christ's resurrection and final coming.
20: The teaching scenario — the son asking "what is the meaning of these testimonies and statutes and rules?" — is a catechism built into the covenant. The NT catechism answers the same question through the gospel story: the law, prophets, and writings all point to Christ (Luke 24:27, 44). When a child asks "what does this mean?" the answer in the new covenant is: "God sent his Son... for our sins" (1 John 4:9–10).
21: "We were Pharaoh's slaves in Egypt, and the LORD brought us out." The creed of Israel is the Exodus confession. The NT creed is structured identically: "you were slaves to sin, and Christ brought you out" (Romans 6:17–18; Colossians 1:13–14). Ephesians 2 uses the same pattern: "you were dead in your trespasses... but God, being rich in mercy... made us alive together with Christ." The catechetical form — from slavery to freedom — shapes both covenants' proclamation.
22: "The LORD showed great and devastating signs and wonders against Egypt." The plagues display YHWH's superiority over Egypt's gods. Christ's miracles operate in the same register: "if it is by the Spirit of God that I cast out demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you" (Matthew 12:28). His signs are not isolated prodigies but the advancing of the kingdom against powers holding people captive — a new series of acts of power against the spiritual Egypt.
23: "He brought us out from there, that he might bring us in and give us the land." The Exodus-to-land structure: liberation from one place always aims at establishment in another. Christ's atonement follows the same shape: "he died for our sins" (liberation) "and was raised for our justification" (entry, Romans 4:25). The new exodus liberates not merely to freedom in the abstract but to the specific inheritance of the new creation (Revelation 21:1–4).
24: "The LORD commanded us to do all these statutes, to fear the LORD our God, for our good always." The commands are not punitive but life-giving — YHWH's law is for Israel's flourishing. Jesus describes his mission in the same terms: "I came that they may have life and have it abundantly" (John 10:10). Law and gospel are aligned at the level of ultimate aim — both are for life — though they differ decisively in mechanism: external command vs. Spirit-transformation.
25: "It will be righteousness for us if we are careful to do all this commandment before the LORD our God." The Torah-righteousness formula: obedience = righteousness. Paul explicitly contrasts this with the righteousness of faith: "not having a righteousness of my own that comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God that depends on faith" (Philippians 3:9). Romans 10:3–6 cites Deuteronomy's righteousness as the approach Christ replaced — not because the law is bad, but because human inability makes Christ's righteousness the only viable path.
